title: Fundamental Groups of Random Simplicial Complexes
speaker: Eric Babson

proof_stages.label: Linial-Meshulam random 2-complex model
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the random 2-dimensional simplicial complex with complete one-skeleton and independent triangular faces included with probability n^{-alpha}, together with the asymptotically almost surely and threshold terminology.

proof_stages.label: Threshold landscape for homology and fundamental groups
proof_stages.summary: Known results are stated: finite-coefficient homology and absence of fixed-index subgroups have threshold alpha=1, while triviality of pi_1 has threshold alpha=1/2, motivating questions about integral homology, finite quotients, residual finiteness, property T, and local subcomplex types.

proof_stages.label: Hyperbolicity and short-loop reduction for the sparse side
proof_stages.summary: For alpha>1/2, the nontriviality direction is reduced to proving a linear isoperimetric inequality for the random complex and then showing that the specific 3-cycle on vertices 1,2,3 cannot have area at most 3 delta, hence represents a nontrivial element of pi_1.

proof_stages.label: Generalized word-complex random group models
proof_stages.summary: The simplex model is placed in a family of word-labeled 2-complexes Delta_n^{a,b}, including relator and density models, and the maps sigma_s are used to compare measures and transport results between models.

proof_stages.label: Other random-space constructions for fundamental groups
proof_stages.summary: The speaker surveys further probabilistic topological constructions—cones on graphs, Rips complexes, random Morse functions, and random gluings—as settings where similar fundamental-group questions could be posed.

proof_stages.label: Local-to-global mechanism for hyperbolicity
proof_stages.summary: The proof of hyperbolicity is refined by combining Gromov's local-global theorem with the fact that random complexes are locally alpha-admissible, then controlling admissible local pieces and disc diagrams to obtain a hyperbolicity constant depending only on alpha.

examples.note: The speaker notes the first local copy of RP^2, with opposite faces identified, to illustrate how local two-torsion can appear before the global fundamental group is trivial.
